title: Testing Resolve in US-Iran Negotiations
content:
During a tense 21-hour diplomatic marathon, the United States and Iran met to test one another's resolve after nearly forty days of fighting. The US delegation, led by Vice President JD Vance, included nearly 300 officials who presented a comprehensive 100-page explanation on nuclear facility safety. The Iranian negotiating team featured seasoned diplomats such as Ali Bagheri Kani and Abbas Araghchi, both of whom had extensive experience from previous nuclear talks. Despite the high stakes, reaching a comprehensive agreement in such a short time proved unrealistic. Issues that previously required two years of careful negotiation between 2013 and 2015 could not be resolved in a single session.

Throughout the talks, Vance reportedly spoke with Donald Trump at least a dozen times and held a crucial conversation with Israel's Benjamin Netanyahu. Iranian negotiators claimed that this communication led to a noticeable hardening of the US position. Prior to the meeting, Iran had already compromised slightly by not securing its demanded full ceasefire in Lebanon or the release of its frozen assets. Meanwhile, Trump's stated alternative plan involved blockading the Strait of Hormuz to prevent Iranian oil exports, a move that diplomats warned would only increase global oil prices. Ultimately, veterans of previous nuclear negotiations noted that twenty-one hours was far too short if the actual goal was to reach a meaningful compromise.
